poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
GTM Ops becomes easier to understand when you look at what it helps the business do.

It sits across teams and supports how the revenue system actually runs.

That can include:
• making sure leads go to the right place
• keeping pipeline data usable
• aligning definitions across functions
• improving handoffs
• reducing friction in the workflow

A lot of this work happens in the background. When it is done well, you can feel the difference. Teams move faster. Ownership feels clearer. Reporting becomes more useful. Problems become easier to diagnose.

It sits close to the operating layer of the business and that is part of what makes the role so interesting. 
#saas #GTM

poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
Status as of today: AI shows up across GTM teams in useful and practical ways.

Not as a dramatic replacement for work. More as support inside the workflow.

You will see it helping with things like:
• summarizing notes
• enriching lead context
• drafting first-pass communication
• reducing repetitive admin work
• organizing information faster

What makes these use cases valuable is that they save time inside real operating systems.

That matters, especially in teams where small efficiency gains can improve handoffs, speed and focus.

The real value tends to show up when AI is layered into a process that already has some structure behind it. It works best inside a clear system because AI improves the execution, it does not replace structure (yet :)). 
#Saas #GTM

poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
What deserves attention?
What needs context? 
What is "actually" urgent? 
What matters commercially? 
What a signal means beyond the numbers?

These are the things which a human needs to decide. 
A system can surface data, 
AI can organize information, 
and AI agents can reduce repetitive tasks. 
But the answers to the above questions in Saas GTM still depend heavily on judgment.

And that judgement/decision layer is difficult to automate well because good decisions usually depend on tradeoffs. 
They depend on timing, 
experience, 
business context 
and a feel for what matters in that moment!
#GTM #saas

poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
SaaS roles start to make a lot more sense once you see how teams actually work together.

If you are fresh out of college or making a transition from another industry, everything looks clean from outside. Marketing generates leads. Sales closes deals. Ops supports the process.

Inside a real company, it’s far more connected.

Marketing influences lead quality.
Lead quality shapes sales conversations.
Sales feedback informs future campaigns.
Ops makes the systems usable and measurable.

A lot of the real work happens in the connections:

• aligning teams
• handling imperfect data
• managing handoffs
• making decisions with partial information

Understanding a role in isolation only gets you so far.

It starts to make more sense when you see the end to end system it operates within.
#saas #GTM

poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
AI is revealing which parts of GTM work actually create value.

That is one of the most interesting shifts happening right now. Some work is getting faster. Some work is getting easier. And some work is being exposed as low-value repetition.

That creates a useful kind of pressure. It pushes teams to think more clearly about what really matters in the workflow.

The real edge is starting to come from knowing where judgment matters, where context matters and where process design matters.

That is why the conversation around AI gets more useful when it moves beyond tools.
#saas #GTM

poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
A demo booking looks like progress on a dashboard.

In reality, it’s the start of a much more fragile process.

Once the form is filled, things begin to move quietly.

The lead gets routed.
Someone decides if it’s worth pursuing.
A rep reaches out.
Bits of context are pieced together.
An opportunity may take shape or fade out early.

Nothing here feels dramatic.

But small differences start to matter.

A quicker response builds rhythm.
A delayed one breaks it.
Clear context sharpens the conversation.
Gaps in it create friction.

Individually, these moments are easy to overlook.
Across a big pipeline, they define outcomes.

That’s why looking only at lead volume rarely explains growth.

The more telling signal is how the system handles what comes in.

Because once a lead enters the system, the experience is no longer accidental. It’s designed, whether intentionally or not.

And that design shows up in results!

How that flow works is usually where the real story is.
#saas #GTM

poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
There’s a layer behind B2B SaaS growth that rarely gets talked about.

It sits between marketing and sales, and quietly shapes how revenue actually moves.

On the surface, growth can look simple.
Marketing brings in leads. Sales works the pipeline. Revenue follows.

But underneath that, there is a system handling everything in between:

- how leads get routed
- how they get qualified
- how quickly someone follows up
- how clean the data is
- how information moves from one team to another

When this layer is not clear, everything feels harder than it should.

The issue is not always effort. More often, growth problems are system architecture and process problems.

That’s why understanding the operating layer matters so much in SaaS.

Once you see the system, the outcomes stop feeling random.
#saas #GTM

attributes.headline: Education Management Professional

poster.headline: AI First Leader - Go-to-market Strategy & Operations | Saas

data.text:
Heard a simple but powerful story recently. Full credit and thanks to Sangeeta Rautji ma'am.

A carrot, an egg and coffee beans are placed into three pots of boiling water.

After some time,
The carrot, which began firm, becomes soft.
The egg, once fragile, becomes hard inside.
The coffee beans, however, do something entirely different. They transform the water itself into coffee and notably spread their aroma.

Same situation but different responses.

It’s a useful way to think about how we show up under pressure. A good reminder that how we respond tends to matter as much as (if not more than) the situation itself.

poster.headline: CEO @nRev | Making AI Automation building effortless for GTM teams | IIT Kharagpur

data.text:
I turned 3,000 LinkedIn engagements into pipeline.
(without sending a single cold message)

For 90 days, I tracked every piece of LinkedIn engagement at nRev.

3,000+ people engaged with our content. Profile views, likes, comments, shares.

Less than 4% ever became a conversation.

Not because the content was bad.
Because we had zero system for what happens after someone engages.

That 96% that slipped through? That was $1.8M+ in warm pipeline walking away.

So I built the system.

I spent 90 days:
→ Mapping every warm signal LinkedIn gives you for free
→ Testing 3 automation workflows across real ICP accounts
→ Failing at manual follow-ups and documenting what scaled
→ Studying how top revenue teams turn engagement into pipeline

Then I ran it on our own GTM motion and with B2B SaaS customers.

Now I'm giving away the entire playbook.

Here's the truth: The LinkedIn algorithm is already doing its job. It's showing your posts to your buyers. They're liking. They're viewing your profile. They're tagging your company.

You're just not capturing the signal.

The warm intent window is 48 hours. After that, your prospect moved on. Your competitor's system already reached out.

This free playbook includes:
✅ The 3 warm signals most GTM leaders completely ignore
✅ 3 copy-paste automation workflows (2-4 steps each)
✅ Outreach templates for each signal type that actually convert
✅ The 20-reaction threshold filter that separates prospects from noise
✅ Speed-to-lead math showing why Hour 1 response = 391% higher conversion
✅ A 3-week implementation checklist you can start today
✅ Common mistakes that kill warm outreach before it starts

Warm networks convert 3-5x better than cold outreach. Every single time.

No fluff. No $5k course. Just the system I wish I had 12 months ago.

Want it?

👉 Comment "SIGNAL" below, connect with me and I'll send it over

🔄 Repost for early access

poster.headline: Partner at Morgan Executive Search LLC

data.text:
I’m seeing a quiet shift in PE-backed SaaS hiring right now.

The “hero CRO” search is getting replaced by something more unglamorous, and more effective:

Revenue Operations leadership with real authority.

Why?

Because boards are realizing the risk is not “we need a better pitch.”
The risk is “we do not know what is actually true in the funnel.”

When AI and automation start changing productivity assumptions, the old operating model breaks.
Even Deloitte is calling out agentic AI as a 2026 pressure point that forces new approaches for software companies. 

So the question I’m hearing in interviews is changing:

Less:
Can you grow?

More:
Can you make growth measurable, repeatable, and financeable?

What that looks like in real life:
	1.	One definition of pipeline
No more Salesforce vs spreadsheet vs “my number” debates.
	2.	Forecasting that survives scrutiny
Not optimism. Not sandbagging. A model you can explain.
	3.	Pricing and packaging discipline
Especially as per-user models get challenged and buyers demand ROI clarity. 
	4.	Sales capacity you can trust
Territories, ramp, coverage, and conversion that match the plan.

If you are a CEO or Operating Partner, here’s the litmus test:

If your CRO left tomorrow, would you still believe the number?

If the answer is “I’m not sure,” you do not have a CRO problem.
You have an operating system problem.

And that is why RevOps is moving from “support function” to “core leadership hire” in 2026.

data.resharer_comment: Worrisome for businesses that thrive on the basis of communities. #AI
data.text:
Stack Overflow has lost 98% of its users to AI. No one misses it.

In 2014, developers asked 200,000 questions per month on Stack Overflow.

In December 2025, that number was 3,862.

What happened? Developers switched to AI tools like GitHub Copilot directly in their IDEs. Why wait hours for an answer when AI gives you one in seconds?

But here's the thing: AI didn't kill Stack Overflow. Stack Overflow killed itself.

For years, the platform was notorious for hostile moderation, condescending replies, and questions marked as "duplicate" of threads from 2011 with outdated answers that no longer work.

Developers put up with it because there was no alternative.

Now there is.

84% of developers now use or plan to use AI coding tools. And when they made the switch, they didn't look back.

Stack Overflow built one of the most valuable knowledge bases in tech history. Then they let their community become so toxic that everyone left the moment something better came along.

There's a lesson here for any platform that thinks their users have no choice.

poster.headline: Managing Director and VP - Avalara India and APAC |. Vice President - GTM @ Avalara APAC I AI-First CRO I GCC leadership | Cloud | FinTech | CX | Industry Solutions I Education

data.text:
Three mistakes Indian startups make while doing business in the US. 


1)  First mistake is delaying sales tax registration. Many founders believe they must set up a U.S. entity before registering, but most states trigger the obligation based on sales thresholds, not corporate structure.

2) The second is misclassifying products or services. Incorrect HS codes can change duty treatment and delay shipments. For SaaS companies, inaccurate classification of digital services can lead to incorrect tax collection because each state defines taxability differently.

3) The third is relying too heavily on payment processors or marketplaces. These platforms may calculate tax at checkout, but they do not manage registration, filing or remittance for the seller. Marketplace facilitator laws require large platforms to collect and remit tax on marketplace transactions, yet sellers must still maintain accurate records and comply with filing rules. The responsibility for compliance remains with the business.

https://lnkd.in/ggr4ZDbP

Avalara Avalara APAC. Inc42 Media 
#IndiaStartups
#SaaS 
#ExportstoUSA
#Compliance
